perturbation.original_text: The Company shall maintain the effectiveness of the Eligible Registration Statement until the earlier of (i) the date on which all Registrable Securities included in such Eligible Registration Statement have actually been sold and (ii) the date that is (x) 180 days (in respect of a Resale Shelf Registration Statement) or (y) 90 days (in respect of any Eligible Registration Statement other than a Resale Shelf Registration Statement) from the effective date of such Eligible Registration Statement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will endeavor to maintain the effectiveness of the Eligible Registration Statement until what it deems the reasonable earlier of (i) the date on which all Registrable Securities included in such Eligible Registration Statement have actually been sold and (ii) approximately the date that is (x) 180 days (in respect of a Resale Shelf Registration Statement) or (y) 90 days (in respect of any Eligible Registration Statement other than a Resale Shelf Registration Statement) from the effective date of such Eligible Registration Statement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain' to 'will endeavor to maintain' weakens the obligation. The addition of 'what it deems the reasonable earlier of' adds subjectivity. The term 'approximately' further reduces the certainty of the deadline.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 415 requires continuous offering, and this modification allows for ambiguous discontinuation of the registration's effectiveness, hindering the ability to resell securities, thus contradicting SEC regulations. The 'endeavor' and 'deems reasonable' language suggests the company might not fulfill its obligations to keep the registration effective for the required duration, potentially affecting the investor's ability to sell their shares.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 230.415 - Delayed or continuous offering and sale of securities. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.415 - Delayed or continuous offering and sale of securities. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.415 Delayed or continuous offering and sale of securities. (a) Securities may be registered for an offering to be made on a continuous or delayed basis in the future, Provided, That: (1) The registration statement pertains only to: (i) Securities which are to be offered or sold solely by or on behalf of a person or persons other than the registrant , a subsidiary of the registrant or a person of which the registrant is a subsidiary ; (ii) Securities which are to be offered and sold pursuant to a dividend or interest reinvestment plan or an employee benefit plan of the registrant ; (iii) Securities which are to be issued upon the exercise of outstanding options, warrants or rights; (iv) Securities which are to be issued upon conversion of other outstanding securities; (v) Securities which are pledged as collateral; (vi) Securities which are registered on Form F-6 ( § 239.36 of this chapter); (vii) Asset-backed securities (as defined in 17 CFR 229.1101 (c)) registered (or qualified to be registered) on Form SF-3 ( § 239.45 of this chapter) which are to be offered and sold on an immediate or delayed basis by or on behalf of the registrant ; Instruction to paragraph (a)(1)(vii): The requirements of General Instruction I.B.1 of Form SF-3 ( § 239.45 of this chapter) must be met for any offerings of an asset-backed security (as defined in 17 CFR 229.1101 (c)) registered in reliance on this paragraph (a)(1)(vii). (viii) Securities which are to be issued in connection with business combination transactions; (ix) Securities, other than asset-backed securities (as defined in 17 CFR 229.1101 (c)), the offering of which will be commenced promptly, will be made on a
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the Company 'shall maintain' the effectiveness of the Eligible Registration Statement. The changed text weakens this obligation by stating the Company 'will endeavor to maintain' its effectiveness and introduces subjectivity by adding 'what it deems the reasonable earlier of' and 'approximately'. This contradicts the requirement for continuous offering and sale of securities under Rule 415, as indicated in the Law Explanation and supported by the scraped snippets, specifically 17 CFR § 230.415, which concerns delayed or continuous offering and sale of securities. The change creates an ambiguous legal obligation regarding maintaining the effectiveness of the registration, impacting the investor's ability to resell securities.

perturbation.original_text: The Farids Group shall not (unless specifically requested in writing by the Company, acting through a resolution of a majority of the Company's directors), directly or indirectly, and agrees to cause the Affiliates of the Farid Group to not, directly or indirectly, in any manner acquire, offer or propose to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, whether by purchase, tender or exchange offer, through the acquisition of control of another person, by joining a partnership, limited partnership, syndicate or other group (including any group of persons that would be treated as a single "person" under Section 13(d) of the Exchange Act), through swap or hedging transactions or otherwise, any securities of the Company, or any rights decoupled from the underlying securities of the Company that would result in the Farids Group (together with its Affiliates) owning, controlling or otherwise having any beneficial ownership (within the meaning of Rule 13d-3 under the Exchange Act, or any successor provision) of 19.99% or more of the fully diluted number of shares of Common Stock outstanding at such time
perturbation.changed_text: The Farids Group should not (unless maybe specifically requested in writing by the Company, with potential approval through a resolution of a majority of the Company's directors), directly or indirectly, and sort of agrees to cause the Affiliates of the Farid Group to try to not, directly or indirectly, in any particular manner acquire, offer or propose to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, whether by purchase, tender or exchange offer, through the acquisition of control of another person, by joining a partnership, limited partnership, syndicate or other group (including any group of persons that would be treated as a single "person" under Section 13(d) of the Exchange Act), through swap or hedging transactions or otherwise, more than a small portion of any securities of the Company, or any rights decoupled from the underlying securities of the Company that would result in the Farids Group (together with its Affiliates) owning, controlling or otherwise having any beneficial ownership (within the meaning of Rule 13d-3 under the Exchange Act, or any successor provision) of 19.99% or more of the fully diluted number of shares of Common Stock outstanding at such time
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'should not' and adding words like 'maybe', 'potential', 'sort of agrees', 'try to not', and 'particular' all introduce significant ambiguity, diluting the force of the restriction on acquiring company securities. The vague term 'more than a small portion of any securities' contradicts the stated 19.99% limit.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 13(d) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 13(d) requires disclosure of beneficial ownership exceeding 5%. This modification creates ambiguity that makes it harder to enforce the 19.99% limit. The added vagueness could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the reporting requirements of Section 13(d) by making it unclear when the Farids Group's ownership triggers the reporting obligation. By making compliance discretionary rather than mandatory, it contradicts the intent of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934.

Periodical and other reports (a) Reports by issuer of security; contents Every issuer of a security registered pursuant to section 78l of this title shall file with the Commission, in accordance with such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate for the proper protection of investors and to insure fair dealing in the security- (1) such information and documents (and suc
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the restriction on the Farids Group's ability to acquire company securities. The original text states 'shall not,' indicating a firm prohibition, while the changed text uses 'should not' and adds qualifying words like 'maybe,' 'potential,' 'sort of agrees,' 'try to not,' and 'particular.' This weakens the obligation. Additionally, the phrase 'more than a small portion of any securities' directly contradicts the original 19.99% limit, creating a direct conflict. The law snippets provided relate to the Securities Exchange Act and periodical reports required by issuers, indicating the importance of transparency in securities ownership, as indicated by snippets 1 and 2. These ambiguities introduced, undermines the purpose of Section 13(d) by obscuring when reporting obligations are triggered. This contradiction is direct and significant, making the contract legally questionable.

perturbation.original_text: Each of EA, Farids and the Company shall not, and shall cause their respective controlled Affiliates to not, either directly or indirectly solicit, hire, or contract with any of the employees of the other party or its Affiliates during the Term (as defined in the Exclusive Supplier Operating Agreement) and for one (1) year following the termination or expiration thereof; provided that this Section 8.9(a) shall not apply with respect to any such employee who employment with the other party and its Affiliates has been terminated for a period in excess of nine (9) months.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of EA, Farids and the Company will aim to not, and will encourage their respective possibly controlled Affiliates to attempt to not, either directly or indirectly solicit, hire, or contract with any of the possibly key employees of the other party or its Affiliates during most of the Term (as vaguely defined in the Exclusive Supplier Operating Agreement) and for maybe one (1) year following what appears to be the termination or expiration thereof; provided that this Section 8.9(a) might not apply with respect to any such employee who employment with the other party and its Affiliates has been terminated for potentially a period in excess of nine (9) months.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not' with 'will aim to not' and 'cause' with 'encourage' significantly weakens the obligation. Adding words like 'possibly', 'attempt', 'vaguely defined', 'appears to be', 'might not' and 'potentially' introduce substantial doubt and ambiguity, making the non-solicitation agreement very difficult to enforce.
perturbation.law_explanation: C.R.S. § 8-2-113 addresses agreements not to compete. While this section primarily deals with direct non-compete agreements, the weakening of the non-solicitation agreement could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the spirit of the law. Colorado law requires such restrictions to be reasonable. By rendering the agreement's obligations vague and discretionary, it undermines the reasonableness and enforceability of the restriction, potentially creating a situation where it unfairly restricts employee mobility, conflicting with Colorado's stance against unreasonable restraints on trade.

perturbation.justification: The original text establishes a clear non-solicitation agreement, while the changed text significantly weakens this obligation by introducing ambiguity and discretionary language. The terms 'shall not' and 'cause' are replaced with weaker alternatives like 'will aim to not' and 'encourage,' and qualifiers such as 'possibly,' 'attempt,' and 'potentially' are added. This undermines the enforceability of the agreement, potentially conflicting with the intent of laws against unreasonable restraints on trade, as described in the Law Explanation. The altered language makes the obligations vague and discretionary, directly impacting the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement. LexisNexis snippet does not contain relevant text to the evaluation.
